title: Extracted & Adapted Quiz Items for 8 Lessons
description: Physics collision and force quiz items with confidence checks for cognitive engagement assessment

core_ideas: In a collision between two objects, the objects have to come into contact; sometimes something is damaged, but not always.
Different factors and variables may cause objects to be damaged or not damaged in a collision.

misconceptions.a: If nothing looks damaged after the collision, the force must have been weak.
misconceptions.b: Heavier objects always cause more damage. (not thinking about other factors)

quiz_items.stem: A phone drops onto a soft pillow. The phone looks the same afterward. Which statement best describes the collision?

options.B: Only the phone pushed; the pillow did not.
options.C: The phone and pillow pushed on each other.
options.D: The force was weak because nothing was damaged.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
A bug hits a car windshield while the car is moving on the highway. The windshield looks the same afterward. Which statement best describes the collision?

options.A: The bug and the windshield pushed on each other during contact.
options.B: There was no collision because the windshield did not change shape.
options.C: The windshield exerted a force, but the bug did not because it is small.
options.D: The force must have been weak because the windshield was not damaged.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

A heavy bowling ball hits a window very slowly. A light golf ball hits the same window very fast. The bowling ball does not break the window, but the golf ball broke the window. Which statement best explain it?

options.A: Speed can matter as well as mass.
options.C: The window must have been defective.
options.D: Heavier objects always cause more damage.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
A large truck and a small compact car collide head-on. The car is badly crumpled, but the truck shows much less damage. Which statement best explains why the damage differs?

options.A: The truck must have exerted a much larger force to the car.
options.B: The car was damaged because it exerted no force on the truck.
options.C: The truck was less damaged because smaller objects have less gravity.
options.D: Damage depends on factors such as materials and design of each object, not only mass.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

core_ideas: The motion of an object is determined by the sum of the forces acting on it; if the total force on the object is not zero, its motion will change.
A collision can cause the objects involved to change motion and/or change shape.
Energy transfer occurs during a collision.
There is a force(s) between objects when they make contact during a collision.

misconceptions.1-a: A force is needed to keep something moving at constant speed.
misconceptions.1-b: Balanced forces mean the object must be at rest.
misconceptions.2-a: If motion changes, there is a change in force.
misconceptions.3-a: Energy is lost/disappears in an inelastic ('sticky') collision.
misconceptions.3-b: Momentum and energy are the same (or both are always conserved).

quiz_items.stem: A cart bumps a box at rest on ice. After the bump, the box moves forward at nearly constant speed. Which statement best describes the forces on the box while it is sliding?

options.A: Gravity makes the box move forward after the bump.
options.B: The box must have a forward force on it to keep moving.
options.C: Forces on the box can be balanced while it keeps moving.
options.D: Balanced forces mean the box must be at rest, so it cannot be moving.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
A moving lump of clay hits a block and sticks to it. After the collision, the clay and block are no longer moving. Where did the clay's kinetic energy go?

options.A: It vanished when the objects stopped moving.
options.B: It disappeared because the clay changed shape permanently.
options.C: It stayed as kinetic energy in the clay and block even though they are not moving.
options.D: It was transferred to the clay, block, and surroundings, such as warming up and sound during the collision.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
A cart is pulled on a low-friction track. The spring scale reading stays the same, and the cart speeds up. What does this show?

options.A: A force is only needed to start the cart, not to speed it up.
options.B: The pull force can stay the same while the cart's motion changes.
options.C: The pull force must be increasing because the cart is speeding up.
options.D: The pull force must be decreasing because the cart is getting faster.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
On a nearly frictionless track, a moving cart hits a cart at rest and they stick together. Which statement about the two-cart system is best?

options.A: Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved because the carts stick.
options.B: Total kinetic energy is conserved, but momentum is not because the carts stick.
options.C: Both the total momentum and the total kinetic energy of the system are conserved.
options.D: The total momentum of the system is conserved, but the total kinetic energy of the system decreases.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

core_ideas: All solid objects bend or change shape in a collision and when other contact forces are applied to them.

misconceptions.a: If it bends, it must be damaged (permanent). If it 'looks normal,' no deformation happened.
misconceptions.b: Same force always means the same deformation regardless of area.

quiz_items.stem: A steel ball hits a thick steel plate and bounces. Afterward, the plate looks exactly the same as before. During the moment of collision, did the steel plate deform at all?

options.A: No, steel is too hard to deform.
options.B: Yes, but only the ball deformed; the plate did not.
options.C: No, if it deformed, we would definitely see a dent.
options.D: Yes, the plate deformed a tiny amount during the collision, even if we cannot see it afterward.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
High-speed video shows a golf club hitting a golf ball. The ball looks flattened for a split second, then returns to its original shape. What is the best conclusion?

options.A: The ball's shape change was temporary.
options.B: Solid objects do not change shape when hit.
options.C: The ball flattened on its own because it is soft.
options.D: The ball was damaged because it changed shape.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
Two balls are the same material. One has twice the diameter of the other. You press each ball into soft clay with the same downward force. Which is most likely?

options.A: Neither makes a dent because the balls are solid.
options.B: Both make the same dent because the force is the same.
options.C: The larger ball makes a deeper dent because it has more surface area.
options.D: The smaller ball makes a deeper dent because the contact area is smaller.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

You press a nail into a block of soft wood with the same downward force two times. Trial 1: you press using the flat head of the nail. Trial 2: you press using the sharp tip of the nail. Which is most likely?

options.A: Neither makes a dent because the nail is solid.
options.B: Both make the same dent because the force is the same.
options.C: The sharp tip makes a deeper dent because the same force is applied over a smaller contact area.
options.D: The flat head makes a deeper dent because it touches more wood at once, so it pushes a larger area downward.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

core_ideas: All solid objects deform elastically when force is applied to them, up to a point.
Different objects have a different elastic limit, which is the maximum amount of deformation they can withstand, beyond which they will deform permanently.
Different objects have a different breaking point, which is the maximum amount of deformation they can withstand, beyond which they will crack or split apart.
The type of material, the shape, and the thickness of an object all affect how much it deforms when a force is applied to it.

misconceptions.1-a: If it bends, it must be damaged (permanent). If it 'looks normal,' no deformation happened.
misconceptions.1-b: Same force always means the same deformation regardless of area.
misconceptions.2-a: Elastic limit is an amount of force (or a visible bend), not a material threshold.
misconceptions.3-a: The thicker the object is, the more challenging it is to be broken.
misconceptions.3-b: Breaking point is how much something bends.
misconceptions.4-a: If force applied is not enough, for some objects, they won't deform.

quiz_items.stem: A plastic spoon is bent. At first it springs back. If bent farther, it stays bent. If bent even farther, it snaps. Which statement best matches the terms?

options.A: The elastic limit is when it snaps.
options.B: The breaking point is when it first bends.
options.C: The breaking point is when it stays bent.
options.D: Elastic limit is where it won't spring back; breaking point is where it snaps.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

Two rulers are the same length but made of different materials. You push down on the center of each ruler with the same force and then let go. Ruler A springs back. Ruler B stays slightly bent. Which conclusion is best?

options.B: Ruler B did not deform because the force was not strong enough.
options.C: The same force pushed Ruler B past its elastic limit, so it deformed permanently.
options.D: Elastic limit is the amount of force you used, so both rulers have the same elastic limit.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
You push on a thick wooden table with your finger. The table does not appear to move or change shape. Which statement is scientifically accurate?

options.A: No deformation occurred because the table looks exactly the same as before.
options.B: Deformation only begins once the force is strong enough to leave a permanent dent.
options.C: The table deformed elastically, even if the change was too small for your eyes to see.
options.D: Solid objects like wood are too hard to deform unless they reach their breaking point.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
Two beams are made of the same plastic. Beam X is twice as thick as Beam Y. If you apply the same amount of force to both, which will happen?

options.A: Beam Y deforms more because it is thinner.
options.B: Both deform the same because the same force is applied.
options.C: Neither deforms unless the force is large enough to crack the plastic.
options.D: Beam X deforms more because the same force is applied over a smaller contact area.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

core_ideas: Objects that make contact with each other apply equally strong forces on each other in opposite directions.
Objects that collide apply an equally strong peak force (maximum force) on each other during the collision.
Increasing the speed or mass of a moving object increases its kinetic energy (KE).
The more KE that objects in a system have, the higher the peak forces they can produce in a collision. (It is right only when the time is controlled; like the collision is an 'immediate')

misconceptions.1-a: Heavier object exerts the larger force in a collision.
misconceptions.1-b: Faster object exerts the larger force.
misconceptions.1-c: The 'active' pusher produces more force than the 'passive' object.
misconceptions.2-a: At the moment of impact, the more massive object exerts the larger peak force.
misconceptions.3-a: Increasing KE of an object indicates a larger force applied to the object.
misconceptions.4-a: During a collision, an object with more KE applies a larger peak force than that it receives.

quiz_items.stem: A large delivery truck collides with a small mosquito. During the brief time they are in contact, how do the forces they exert on each other compare?

options.A: They exert forces of equal strength on each other.
options.B: The truck exerts a stronger force on the mosquito.
options.C: The mosquito exerts a stronger force on the truck.
options.D: Neither exerts a force because the mosquito is too small.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

A soccer player (Player A) collides with a stationary player (Player B). In a second trial, Player A runs twice as fast before hitting Player B. In both trials, the collision lasts for the same amount of time. Which statement best explains the forces involved?

options.A: Player A exerts a larger force than Player B in the second collision.
options.B: The peak force is the same in both collisions because Player B is standing still.
options.C: The peak force is larger in the second collision because Player A has more kinetic energy.
options.D: The peak force is smaller in the second collision because faster motion reduces contact time.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
Two students stand on skateboards facing each other. Student A pushes on Student B's hands to start B rolling away. While their hands are in contact, how do the forces compare?

options.A: Student B exerts no force because B is passive.
options.B: Student B exerts a larger force because B is being pushed backward.
options.C: Student A exerts a larger force because A is the one doing the pushing.
options.D: They exert forces of equal strength on each other in opposite directions.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

A moving hockey puck hits a stationary puck of the same mass in a very brief collision. The moving puck has more kinetic energy before impact. During the collision, how do the peak forces on the two pucks compare?

options.A: The peak forces are equal in strength on both pucks.
options.B: The stationary puck exerts a larger peak force because it is not moving.
options.C: Only the moving puck exerts a force because the other puck is stationary.
options.D: The moving puck exerts a larger peak force because it has more kinetic energy.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

core_ideas: Application of concepts from Lessons 1-5.

misconceptions.1: Students may think that in a collision between two players, the bigger or faster player exerts a greater force on the smaller or slower player, rather than both players experiencing equal forces.

quiz_items.stem: Two soccer players collide head-to-head. Player 1 is bigger and faster. Which statement is best about the forces during the collision?

options.A: Only the faster player exerts force.
options.B: Both exert equal forces on each other.
options.C: Player 1 exerts more force on Player 2.
options.D: Player 2 exerts more force on Player 1.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

The same soccer ball hits the same metal goalpost in two trials. In Trial 1, the ball is moving slowly. In Trial 2, the ball is moving much faster. In which trial will the ball flatten more during the impact?

options.A: Trial 1, because slower collisions cause more flattening.
options.B: Same, because the metal goalpost the ball hit is the same in both trials.
options.C: Trial 2, because a faster-moving ball has more kinetic energy at impact.
options.D: The ball's weight matters most, so it should flatten about the same in both trials.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
A fast skater (Player A) bumps into a slower skater (Player B). During the brief contact, how do the forces compare?

options.A: Player A exerts a larger force because A is faster.
options.B: Only Player A exerts a force because A is moving.
options.C: Player B exerts a larger force because B is slower.
options.D: They exert equal forces on each other in opposite directions.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
A larger football player and a smaller football player collide. The smaller player's speed changes more after the collision. What is the best explanation?

options.A: The larger player exerted a larger force on the smaller player.
options.B: The smaller player exerted no force because they were smaller.
options.C: The larger player felt no force because they kept moving forward.
options.D: The forces were equal in size, but the smaller player's motion changed more because of differences in mass.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

core_ideas: The more kinetic energy an object has the more damage it can do in a collision.
The more kinetic energy an object has the more you have to push against the direction of its motion to get it to stop.
The kinetic energy of an object is directly proportional to its mass; the KE of an object is proportional to the square of its speed.

misconceptions.a: Kinetic energy depends on mass only.
misconceptions.b: Double of speed doubles kinetic energy.
misconceptions.d: An object has energy within it that is used up as the object moves.

quiz_items.stem: Car A and Car B have the same mass. Car B is moving twice as fast as Car A. How does the kinetic energy of Car B compare to Car A?
How confident are you about your answer?
Cart A has mass 2 kg and moves at 2 m/s. Cart B has mass 1 kg and moves at 4 m/s. Which cart has more kinetic energy?

options.A: Cart A, because it has more mass.
options.B: Cart B, because it is moving faster.
options.C: You can't tell without knowing how far each cart travels.
options.D: They have the same kinetic energy because one has double the mass and the other has double the speed.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
Two carts move toward identical foam barriers. Both carts have the same momentum, but Cart A is heavier and moving slower, while Cart B is lighter and moving faster. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Cart A has more kinetic energy because it has more mass.
options.B: You cannot compare kinetic energy unless you know the stopping time.
options.C: They have the same kinetic energy because their momentum is the same.
options.D: Cart B has more kinetic energy because higher speed increases kinetic energy more than mass does.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
A puck glides across very smooth ice at a nearly constant speed for several seconds. Which statement best describes what happens to the puck's kinetic energy while it keeps moving at the same speed?

options.A: It becomes momentum, so kinetic energy is gone.
options.B: It stays about the same because its speed stays about the same.
options.C: It increases because moving objects build up kinetic energy over time.
options.D: It decreases because the puck uses up its kinetic energy to keep moving.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

core_ideas: The more force you apply to an object the more that object speeds up.
It takes more force to speed up a more massive object the same amount as a lower mass object.
Potential energy can be stored in some systems when you change the shape or arrangement of parts in that system (e.g., a spring).
Contact forces transfer energy between different objects or subsystems within the larger cart-launcher system.

misconceptions.1&2-a: Force determines speed, not acceleration (constant force, constant speed).
misconceptions.1&2-b: Motion implies force (object in motion has a forward force).
misconceptions.3-a: Elastic PE does not depend on amount of deformation.
misconceptions.3-b: Reversed dependence with stretch (e.g., a rubber band stretched farther has less elastic energy).
misconceptions.4-a: Energy 'used up' or 'lost,' not transformed to thermal/sound/internal energy during contact.
misconceptions.4-b: If stays in system, energy will be conserved.

quiz_items.stem: A cart is launched by the same spring. Trial 1 compresses the spring 2 cm. Trial 2 compresses it 5 cm. Which trial gives the cart more kinetic energy right after launch?
How confident are you about your answer?

Two carts start from rest on the same low-friction track. You push each cart with the same constant force for the same amount of time. Cart A has a smaller mass than Cart B. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: The heavier cart speeds up more because it has more mass.
options.B: Both carts reach the same speed because the force is the same.
options.C: The cart that ends up moving faster must have had a larger force.
options.D: The lighter cart speeds up more because the same force changes its motion more.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

A cart is launched from a spring and is now rolling across a flat low-friction track. The spring is no longer touching the cart. Which statement best describes the horizontal forces acting on the cart while it is coasting?

options.A: The forward force of the launch is still "inside" the cart, pushing it forward.
options.B: There is a constant forward force from the spring that keeps the cart moving.
options.C: There are no horizontal forces acting on the cart, so it continues at a constant speed.
options.D: The force of the launch must be greater than the force of the air for the cart to keep moving.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

A spring launches a cart. After the launch, the spring is at rest and the cart is moving. If you measure the energy, the cart has slightly less kinetic energy than the potential energy that was originally stored in the compressed spring. Where did the "missing" energy go?

options.A: The energy was used up by the spring to overcome its own weight.
options.B: Some energy was transferred into thermal (heat) and sound energy during the contact.
options.C: The energy disappeared because energy is only conserved in perfectly "ideal" systems.
options.D: The missing energy is still in the spring because springs cannot transfer all their energy.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
